description: Modifying a PostgreSQL ENUM type used in a subscriptions table. The application is mid-deployment with rolling traffic. Review what works, what doesn't, and what's downright dangerous.
review_checklist: Identify which ENUM operations are supported in PostgreSQL
Verify ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE transaction semantics
Check ENUM RENAME and DROP support
Audit value-range gaps and ordering on ENUM values

ground_truth.description: ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE 'paused' cannot be run inside a transaction block (PG limitation, until very recent versions added partial support). Migration tools that wrap changes in transactions silently fail or error. Run this statement OUTSIDE any explicit transaction. Note: PG 12+ allows ADD VALUE in a transaction, BUT the new value cannot be USED in the same transaction (it isn't visible until commit) — meaning the UPDATE on line 11 referencing 'paused' could fail if bundled with line 4.

ALTER TYPE ... RENAME VALUE works, but in a rolling deployment, OLD application instances still emit 'incomplete' for new INSERTs while the migration is running. After the rename, those INSERTs fail with 'invalid input value for enum'. Sequence: deploy new code that handles BOTH 'incomplete' and 'pending_payment' → run RENAME → deploy code that uses only 'pending_payment'. The migration as written breaks rolling deployment.

PostgreSQL DOES NOT SUPPORT `ALTER TYPE ... DROP VALUE`. This statement will fail with a syntax error. To remove an enum value, you must: (1) update all rows that use it (line 11 attempts this but is wrong, see MIG-323), (2) recreate the type without the value, (3) cast every column using the type to the new type. Cumbersome multi-step migration with table rewrites.

UPDATE migrating 'past_due' → 'paused' is the wrong semantic — past_due and paused are different states (past_due = payment failed but still active; paused = explicitly paused by user). This data migration silently corrupts billing reporting. Audit data-mapping decisions with stakeholders.

Architectural critique: ENUM in PostgreSQL is rigid by design — adds are append-only, deletes are unsupported, renames are dangerous in rolling deploys. For state machines that evolve, prefer a small reference table `subscription_statuses(code VARCHAR, label VARCHAR, is_active BOOLEAN, sort_order INTEGER)` with a FK from subscriptions.status. Adding/deprecating values becomes a row INSERT/UPDATE.

subscription_events stores from_status and to_status as the same enum type. After a rename, historical events still reference the OLD value semantically (pre-rename) but the type now stores 'pending_payment'. Audit log integrity broken: querying 'how many subscriptions transitioned out of incomplete state' becomes ambiguous. Append-only audit logs should use VARCHAR with the historical label preserved as text.
